label: John Chrysostom, homilies (Cambridge, University Library, MS Nn.1.22)
description:
This manuscript, probably copied in the 11th century, contains a selection of works of John Chrysostom, homilies from various series delivered by the Patriarch of Constantinople and leading patristic theologian (d. 407). It was originally the latter part of a large manuscript later divided into two parts. The other part is now MS Nn.1.21. One folio which became detached from this portion has been erroneously bound into that volume.

Notes in Nn.1.21 indicate that during the early modern period the original, undivided manuscript was owned by one Ioannes Porphiros Chalasophoures, who together with his wife Anastasia Sophelia donated it to the Monastery of the Panagia Pantochara. This fortified monastery is located on the tiny island of Stamfani, larger of the two remote islands of the Strofades or Strofadia, to the south of Zakynthos. It has since been rededicated to the Transfiguration of Christ and St Dionysios of Zakynthos (1547-1622), once its abbot.

Dr Christopher Wright

value.en: The manuscript originally formed part of a single volume with   MS Nn.1.21 . It was probably copied during the 11th century, on the basis of the style of script.

Owned by Ioannes Porphiros Chalasophoures, and donated by him and his wife Anastasia Sophelia to the Monastery of the Panagia Pantochara, Strophades (founded 1241), in the Ionian Islands, Greece, as indicated by his notes in   MS Nn.1.21 , f. i verso.  Owned by Anthony Askew (1722-1774).
Codex iii + 166 + iii Leaf height: 327 mm, width: 232 mm.
The Polonsky Foundation
11th century
Cambridge, University Library, MS Nn.1.22

The scribe has placed marginal homily numbers alongside the beginning of each homily, forming a single sequence continuing that found in   MS Nn.1.21 , and marginal ὅρα and ση(μείωσαι) marks to highlight notable passages of text (e.g. ff.  15v ,  21v ,  29v ). At the beginning of each homily a later hand has placed homily numbers identical to those used by the original scribe in the upper margin, while another hand has placed counts of the number of folios in each homily here. There are brief marginal annotations in Greek by various later hands. A hand using black ink has overwritten faded original text in places.
Christian literature, Early--Greek authors; Theology, Christian
Dionysios, Saint, 1547-1622; Sophelia, Anastasia; Farmer, Richard, 1735-1797
Codex
Greek

18th-century full leather binding over couched-laminate boards, with marbled pastedowns and outer endleaves. The edges have been marbled in the same pattern as the pastedowns and endleaves. There are decoratve gold-tooled borders on both boards, with a crown motif, and gold tooling on the edges of the boards. The spine bears extensive decorative gold tooling, the classmark on labels, and the title "Chrysostomi varia" and "Tom. II. M.S." tooled in gold on black leather labels.   Binding height: 350 mm, width: 263 mm, depth: 62 mm.

The fore-edge margins of ff.  17  and  49  have been cut away. There are occasional small tears to the edges, mainly in the last few folios ( ff. 163-166 ). There is significant discoloration to the last few folios, and some dirt and drops of wax elsewhere. Erasure of a word on  f. 60r  has weakened the parchment and resulted in holes through this folio. Small areas of text have occasionally become abraded, and these have sometimes been overwritten.

The manuscript was copied in a  mixed minuscule Perlschrift , slanting slightly to the right, in brown ink, with limited variation in letter size and some flourishing of tails into the lower margin. The same hand has added the headings, in  Alexandrine majuscule  script, in brown ink.  Syllabic abbreviations are not in use, apart from the horizontal stroke for  nu , which appears only at the end of lines, while superscript word endings are also not used. Breathings are both angular and round and mute  iota  is usually absent but occasionally adscript. Use of the double dot is functional only, and nomina sacra are rarely accentuated, and usually only when more than three letters long or with enclitics. The double grave accent is in use. Circumflexes very rarely appear above breathings. Nomina sacra strokes are often decorated with an X-shape. Marginal quotation marks of different types are used.  Most majuscule forms are in use in the minuscule text, but not  mu ,  upsilon  or  omega . The long-bowed  beta  is present, with a straight back and often angular loops.  Punctuation used includes the lower, middle and upper points, lower comma, question mark and full stop. The lower comma is rarely used, small pauses being indicated mostly by the lower point.
Cambridge University Library

This manuscript, probably copied in the 11th century, contains a selection of works of  John Chrysostom, homilies  from various series delivered by the Patriarch of Constantinople and leading patristic theologian (d. 407). It was originally the latter part of a large manuscript later divided into two parts. The other part is now   MS Nn.1.21 . One folio which became detached from this portion has been erroneously bound into that volume.  Notes in Nn.1.21 indicate that during the early modern period the original, undivided manuscript was owned by one Ioannes Porphiros Chalasophoures, who together with his wife Anastasia Sophelia donated it to the Monastery of the Panagia Pantochara. This fortified monastery is located on the tiny island of Stamfani, larger of the two remote islands of the Strofades or Strofadia, to the south of Zakynthos. It has since been rededicated to the Transfiguration of Christ and St Dionysios of Zakynthos (1547-1622), once its abbot.  Dr Christopher Wright
Chalasophoures, Ioannes Porphiros; Askew, Anthony, 1722-1774
John Chrysostom, homilies
Christopher Wright
Parchment ( FHHF ), of medium thickness and quite good quality.

The manuscript consists of 21 quires, all of which are quaternia.    Quires 1-20 8   ff. 1-160   Quire 21 8-2   ff. 161-166  The first and last folios have been lost, with loss of text.    There are early quire signatures in Greek numerals, in brown ink, at the end of each quire, verso, lower right, probably original to the production of the manuscript. Some have been lost to cropping, though in these cases traces of their decoration survive. This sequence evidently began from number 25, reflecting the existence of what is now the separate first volume. They survive in whole or in part on Quires 7-20 (καʹ-μδʹ).  There are also modern quire signatures in Greek numerals at the beginning of each quire, in pencil, recto, lower right. The numbers are the same as those of the first sequence, and have been corrected. They appear on all quires.

After Askew's death, the manuscript was sold by G. Leigh and J. Sotheby in the auction of his library in 1785 (MS Nn.4.2, lot 613), and eventually bought by Richard Farmer for Cambridge University Library.

Each homily begins with a major initial in brown ink, usually slightly ornamented. Enlarged minor initials in brown ink mark the beginning of paragraphs. Very simple vignettes, line-fillers and other basic ornament in brown ink accompany the beginning and end of homilies. Quatrefoil plaits in brown ink outline, sometimes with red paint infill, have been used to highlight certain points in the text (ff.  105v ,  115v ,  120v ,  122v ,  155r ). A rough outline for an image, possibly of a head with a halo, has been placed on  f. 106v , probably by a later hand, but has not been completed.
Two columns of 30 lines. Written height: 242 mm, width: 157 mm. Pricking does not survive. Ruled in hardpoint, system Leroy 1, type Leroy 31C2a. Text hangs from the lines or is written along them.
The manuscript is foliated with the numbers [i-iii] + 1-166 + [iv-vi], in black ink, in Arabic numerals, recto, upper right.
Diktyon 76566
Numerous small marker slips of paper have been inserted loose between folios at the head of the manuscript.
